abstract: This article examines the cultures of revolutionary militancy that emerged in the public spaces of France, Italy, and West Germany during the "red decade" (1968–1979). It argues that while radical movements across these borders shared common strategic frameworks and discourses on "counter-violence" and "self-defense," their practical expressions of militancy diverged significantly along national lines. Through a transnational and comparative lens, the study identifies several domestic factors that shaped these different trajectories: the historical legacies of anti-fascist resistance, the specific intensity of local social and industrial conflicts, and the varying degrees of provocation from neo-fascist groups. Central to this divergence was the role of the state; the article demonstrates how domestic policing strategies directly conditioned the level of force adopted by protestors. In particular, the frequent use of lethal force by Italian police fostered a unique environment where the revolutionary left endorsed firearms, blurring the lines between mass protest and armed struggle. By incorporating the French experience—where robust militancy did not transition into systemic terrorism—the author challenges teleological narratives in terrorism studies, suggesting that the transformation of protestors into armed insurgents was a contingent process heavily influenced by the routine violence of state repression rather than ideology alone.
